context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on development as freedom vs rights-based planning, and I’m kinda stuck on the “added value” part. I think it means development is more than just GDP, but idk how to turn that into a solid argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. But let's push that intuition further: what specifically can a person *do* or *be* that GDP wouldn't tell you anything about? Try to think of a concrete example.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a starting intuition (development is more than GDP) but hasn't yet connected it to the specific concept of 'development as freedom' from Sen's framework. Rather than scaffolding the whole argument, I should ask a pointed Socratic question that pushes them to sharpen their intuition and think about what 'freedom' specifically adds that GDP doesn't capture. This keeps the exchange bite-sized and student-driven.

exchanges.student: maybe like being able to go to school, get healthcare, or speak politically without fear? GDP could go up and people still not really have those freedoms, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, if those things - education, healthcare, political voice - are what development should actually be about, what does that imply for how a planner should set priorities or measure success? How does that change the job of planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track, identifying concrete capabilities/freedoms that GDP misses. This is essentially the core of Sen's 'capabilities approach.' Rather than confirming and moving on, I should push them to articulate *why* this matters for development planning specifically - which is what the prompt is really asking about. This moves the Socratic dialogue toward the 'added value' argument they need to build.

exchanges.student: I think it means planning can’t just chase growth stats anymore; it has to ask whether people’s real choices and capabilities improved. I’m not fully sure how to say the “job of planning” part tho.
exchanges.tutor: The phrasing is yours to work out - trust your instincts, you're closer than you think. But before polishing that, let me ask: how is what you just described different from, or related to, *rights-based* development planning? Are they the same thing, or is there a distinction worth making?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a solid conceptual grasp but is now asking me to help them phrase it - which would violate the rule against editing or suggesting language. I should redirect them to own the writing while nudging them toward the next conceptual step: connecting this to the rights-based planning part of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re exactly the same, but I’m fuzzy on the difference rn. Maybe development as freedom is the broad goal, and rights-based planning is more like one way to turn that into policy?
exchanges.tutor: That's an interesting distinction. So if rights-based planning is the operational side, what does framing something as a *right* actually add? What changes - practically or politically - when you say someone has a *right* to education versus just saying education is good for development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a reasonable distinction - freedom as a broad philosophical goal vs. rights-based planning as an operational/policy mechanism. This is a genuinely good insight worth pressing on rather than just validating. I should push them to think about what 'rights' specifically add or constrain that 'freedom' alone doesn't, to sharpen the distinction further before they write.

exchanges.student: I think calling it a right makes the state more accountable, like it’s not just a nice policy goal anymore. But ngl I’m not sure who exactly can claim that right in the “right to development” part.
exchanges.tutor: Good - that accountability point is key. Now on the 'who' question: are we talking about individuals claiming rights, or could whole countries or communities be the claimants? And who would they be claiming those rights *against*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong point about accountability and is now naturally moving toward the fourth part of the prompt: 'who gets to exercise the right to development?' This is great progression. I should acknowledge the accountability insight briefly and then push them to think harder about the 'who' question, which has layers - individuals, communities, nations - especially in the context of non-Western/global power dynamics covered in the course.

exchanges.student: maybe both? like individuals could claim basic freedoms from their state, but poorer countries or communities could also claim fairer global conditions from richer states or institutions, I think.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sophisticated point - you're identifying two different scales of rights claims. Here's a harder question though: does having all these different claimants and obligations actually make planning *easier* or *harder*? Think about what a planner on the ground would have to navigate.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sophisticated multi-scalar understanding of rights claims - individual vs. collective/national, and domestic vs. global. This touches on a core tension in the course around external actors and power. I should affirm the direction and now push them to think about whether this creates any tension or complication for development planning - nudging toward a more critical, nuanced reflection rather than just a descriptive one. This also prepares them to address the 'will it help or hinder planning' part of the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: it prob makes planning harder tbh, cuz now planners have to balance growth, participation, accountability, and even global power issues at once. Is that basically the “hinder” side of the prompt?
exchanges.tutor: That covers the hinder side well. But what about the 'help' side? Can you think of any ways that framing development as freedom or rights actually *makes* planning more effective or legitimate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the 'hinder' side well. But the prompt asks whether it will 'help OR hinder' - implying they should take a position or at least address both sides. I should push them to consider the 'help' side too, so their reflection is more analytically complete. We're also about 7 exchanges in, so I should start nudging toward synthesis and writing.

exchanges.student: yeah I think it helps by making planning more people-centered and legit, since success is about actual lives not just output numbers. It prob also helps planners see who’s being excluded, but idk how to make that sound less vague.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have strong material covering all parts of the prompt now. The vagueness issue will resolve itself when you put it into concrete sentences - writing forces clarity. I'd suggest starting a draft. What would your opening argument be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all four parts of the prompt and has solid raw material. Rather than continuing to build conceptually, this is the right moment to push them toward actually writing - they have enough to produce a draft. The 'vagueness' concern is something they should work out in their own writing rather than have me resolve for them. I should encourage them to start drafting and stop over-preparing.

exchanges.student: maybe my opening claim is that thinking about development as freedom adds value because it redefines development around human capabilities and dignity, not just economic growth, but it also makes planning more demanding. Does that sound focused enough for the intro?
exchanges.tutor: That captures the tension well and hits the prompt directly - it's a solid conceptual foundation. Now stop checking with me and write it. Come back when you have a draft and we can discuss what's working or what needs more development.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate whether their opening claim is focused enough - this is borderline asking for validation/editing. I should not write or polish for them, but I can give honest evaluative feedback on the conceptual strength of what they've described and push them to just start writing it. We are at exchange 9 and the student has all the material they need.

exchanges.student: ok, I’ll draft it rn — I think my main point is that freedom broadens what counts as development, and rights make that politically enforceable but also messier for planners.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp, well-balanced thesis - you've earned it through your own thinking. Go write the draft and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a clear, well-synthesized thesis on their own through the Socratic process. This is exactly the goal. I should briefly affirm this and send them off to write without further scaffolding. The conversation has reached a natural conclusion point.
